title: Amos 1
subtitle: The Lion Roars: Oracles Against the Nations

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Ministry of Amos

heb.paragraph: Not a simple shepherd (roʿeh) but a noqed, a term used elsewhere only of King Mesha of Moab (2 Kgs 3:4). The word suggests a livestock owner of some means, not a landless hireling. Amos was an agricultural businessman, not a destitute peasant.

The verb describes a lion's roar, specifically the roar of a lion about to attack its prey. YHWH's voice from Zion is not a whisper of comfort but a terrifying war cry. The agricultural imagery of pastures withering under this voice connects divine judgment to the world Amos knew intimately.

refs.note: 'The LORD will roar from Zion' — identical language, suggesting a shared prophetic tradition of divine war imagery.
The reign of Jeroboam II and the prophecy of Jonah, placing Amos in the same historical moment.

notes.note: Amos was not a trained prophet or a member of a prophetic school. He was a shepherd and farmer whom God called directly. His background gave him the vocabulary of the land: earthquakes, droughts, livestock, harvests. God uses ordinary people for extraordinary purposes.

The LORD roaring from Zion like a lion signals imminent judgment. The withering of pastures from Carmel to the shepherd lands connects divine wrath to agricultural catastrophe — language a shepherd would understand viscerally.

Calvin observes that God chose a herdsman rather than a priest or noble, demonstrating that prophetic authority derives from divine calling, not human credentials. This principle undergirds the Reformation doctrine of the external call.
Calvin interprets the lion's roar as God's voice in judgment, noting that the same God who speaks gently in gospel promises speaks terrifyingly in prophetic warning.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The term noqed ('sheep breeder') appears only here and in 2 Kgs 3:4, where it describes King Mesha of Moab. The Ugaritic cognate nqd refers to a temple official responsible for sacred flocks, though this institutional background may not apply to Amos.

The phrase 'two years before the earthquake' serves as a chronological anchor. Archaeological evidence from Hazor stratum VI indicates a major earthquake c. 760 BC, consistent with the mid-eighth-century dating of Amos' ministry.

stuart.source: Douglas Stuart, Hosea-Jonah, WBC (1987) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Stuart identifies the superscription as editorially composed but historically reliable. The dual dating (Uzziah and Jeroboam II) places Amos at the zenith of Israelite prosperity, making his message of judgment all the more shocking. The earthquake reference functions as both historical marker and theological foreshadowing: the God who shakes the earth through nature will shake Israel through judgment.

Stuart notes that the lion metaphor frames the entire book. The opening roar (1:2) finds its counterpart in the concluding vision of judgment (9:1-4). The withering of Carmel's summit — the most fertile peak in the land — signals total devastation.

andersen_freedman.source: Francis I. Andersen & David Noel Freedman, Anchor Bible — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Andersen and Freedman argue that the superscription is integral to the book's structure, not a later addition. The word noqed connects Amos to the royal livestock economy, suggesting he was a man of substance with access to trade networks and international awareness — which explains his knowledge of foreign nations.

Andersen and Freedman read the opening couplet as a programmatic statement: YHWH's roar from Zion establishes Jerusalem (not Bethel or Samaria) as the true center of divine authority. This is a direct challenge to the northern kingdom's independent cult.

hist.context: The superscription dates Amos precisely: during the reigns of Uzziah of Judah and Jeroboam II of Israel, 'two years before the earthquake.' This earthquake was so devastating it was remembered 250 years later (Zech 14:5) and has been confirmed archaeologically at Hazor. The opening metaphor of YHWH roaring like a lion from Zion establishes the tone: this is a book of judgment, delivered by a God who is neither distant nor passive.

heb.paragraph: The x/x+1 numerical pattern ('for three transgressions ... and for four') is a standard Hebrew rhetorical device indicating a full measure of sin. It does not mean exactly three or four sins but rather an overflowing accumulation. The formula appears eight times in chapters 1-2, creating the book's most recognizable structure.

refs.note: Hazael's atrocities against Gilead, the specific crime condemned in the Damascus oracle.
Another oracle against the Philistine cities, including Gaza, pronouncing doom on the coastal powers.

notes.note: The 'for three ... for four' formula means that the nation's sins have reached the point of no return. God is patient, but his patience has limits. Damascus had threshed Gilead with iron sledges — a reference to extreme cruelty in warfare.
Damascus is condemned for its brutality against Gilead (the Transjordanian Israelite territory). God holds all nations accountable to basic moral standards, not just covenant nations.
Gaza and the Philistine cities are condemned for slave trading — selling entire communities to Edom. Human trafficking violates the image of God in every person.

Calvin notes that God judges pagan nations for violations of natural law, not Mosaic law. The Gentiles have no Torah, but they have conscience and the moral order written into creation. Syria's cruelty exceeds what even natural law permits.
Calvin observes that God's sovereignty extends over all nations, not merely Israel. The Philistines are accountable to a God they do not acknowledge.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The numerical pattern 'for three X, even for four' (al sheloshsha ... w-al arbaʿah) is a graded numerical saying (cf. Prov 30:15-16, 18-19, 21-23, 29-31). The pattern signals completeness: the total of transgressions has been reached.

The 'Valley of Aven' (biqʿat aven) may be a deliberate distortion of the name of the Beqaʿ Valley ('Valley of Wickedness'), and 'Beth Eden' likely refers to Bit-Adini, an Aramean state on the upper Euphrates.

stuart.source: Douglas Stuart, Hosea-Jonah, WBC (1987) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Stuart reads the oracles-against-nations as a systematic prosecution of international war crimes. Each nation is condemned not for religious offenses but for violations of what would now be called humanitarian law: the Geneva Conventions of the ancient world. Damascus is charged with the specific atrocity of using iron threshing sledges on human captives from Gilead.

Stuart emphasizes that Gaza's crime — deporting entire communities for slave trade with Edom — was a form of ethnic cleansing. The 'remnant of the Philistines shall perish' (v. 8) uses covenant-curse language.

andersen_freedman.source: Francis I. Andersen & David Noel Freedman, Anchor Bible — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Andersen and Freedman argue that the eight oracles form a carefully designed literary unit with concentric structure. The outer ring (Damascus-Israel) contains the longest oracles; the inner pairs are shorter. The progression from foreign nations to covenant people is not merely rhetorical but theological: if God judges pagans for violating natural law, how much more will he judge Israel for violating revealed law.

hist.context: The oracles-against-nations sequence is one of the most brilliant rhetorical strategies in prophetic literature. Amos begins with Israel's distant enemies (Damascus, Gaza), moves through closer neighbors, and spirals inward toward Israel itself (2:6-16). Each oracle follows the same formula: 'For three transgressions and for four, I will not relent.' The Israelite audience would have cheered the condemnation of their enemies, unaware that the net was closing around them.

heb.gloss: a covenant of brotherhood / treaty of kinship
heb.paragraph: Tyre's crime was violating a treaty of brotherhood. The Hebrew berit ahim may refer to the alliance between Hiram of Tyre and Solomon (1 Kgs 5:12; 9:13), suggesting that Tyre betrayed a long-standing diplomatic relationship.

refs.note: The treaty between Solomon and Hiram of Tyre — the 'covenant of brotherhood' that Tyre violated.
The most extensive prophetic condemnation of Edom's fraternal treachery, sharing themes with the Amos oracle.
The origins of the Israel-Edom rivalry in the Jacob and Esau narrative.

notes.note: Tyre broke a treaty of brotherhood, likely the alliance with Solomon's kingdom. God holds nations accountable for violating treaties and diplomatic agreements. International commitments carry moral weight.

Edom's sin was pursuing 'his brother with a sword' — unrelenting hostility toward a kinsman nation (Israel, descended from Jacob; Edom from Esau). The phrase 'stifling all compassion' indicates deliberate cruelty beyond what even warfare normally demands.

Ammon ripped open pregnant women in Gilead to expand their territory. This is among the most horrifying war crimes in the ancient record. God does not overlook atrocities, even when committed by nations outside his covenant.

Calvin focuses on Edom's suppression of natural affection: the bond between brothers (Jacob and Esau) should have restrained violence, but hatred overrode kinship. This is a violation of both natural and divine law.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase 'stifled his compassion' (shihet rahamav) uses a verb (shht) that means 'to destroy/corrupt.' Edom actively destroyed the compassion that kinship should have naturally produced — an emphatic statement of moral culpability.

stuart.source: Douglas Stuart, Hosea-Jonah, WBC (1987) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Stuart groups Tyre and Edom together as betrayers of relationship — Tyre of a diplomatic covenant, Edom of a blood kinship. Both crimes are violations of relational obligation, which Stuart sees as the unifying theme of all eight oracles: each nation is condemned for cruelty that violates the bonds of human community.

Stuart notes that Ammon's war crime — ripping open pregnant women — appears elsewhere in prophetic condemnation (Hos 13:16; 2 Kgs 8:12) and was a recognized atrocity in ancient Near Eastern warfare, aimed at destroying the next generation of an enemy population.

andersen_freedman.source: Francis I. Andersen & David Noel Freedman, Anchor Bible — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Andersen and Freedman observe that the formula 'I will send fire' (w-shillahti esh) occurs in all eight oracles, creating a relentless drumbeat of divine judgment. The fire imagery draws on the theophanic tradition: YHWH's presence is fire, and his judgment is the extension of that consuming holiness.

hist.context: The oracles against Tyre and Edom continue the spiral pattern. Tyre, a commercial power, is condemned for covenant treachery; Edom, Israel's 'brother' (descended from Esau), is condemned for perpetual hostility toward a kinsman nation. The Edom oracle is particularly intense: the verb 'stifled all compassion' (shihet rahamav) suggests a deliberate suppression of the natural kinship instinct.

themes.note: Judgment dominates at 10: every oracle announces divine punishment for war crimes. Justice at 9: the oracles prosecute violations of humanitarian norms. Sovereignty at 9: YHWH exercises authority over all nations, not just Israel.

rows.label: vv.9-15
rows.text: Oracles Against Tyre, Edom, and Ammon

lit.note: Chapter 1 establishes the oracles-against-nations sequence that spirals from distant enemies toward Israel. The 'for three ... for four' formula creates a rhetorical pattern that the audience would have found satisfying — until it turned on them in chapter 2.

hebtext.note: Not a simple shepherd (roʿeh) but a noqed, a term used elsewhere only of King Mesha of Moab (2 Kgs 3:4). The word suggests a livestock owner of some means, not a landless hireling. Amos was an agricultural businessman, not a destitute peasant.

The x/x+1 numerical pattern ('for three transgressions ... and for four') is a standard Hebrew rhetorical device indicating a full measure of sin. It does not mean exactly three or four sins but rather an overflowing accumulation. The formula appears eight times in chapters 1-2, creating the book's most recognizable structure.

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Lion Roars: Oracles Against the Nations

translations.text: This is what the Lord says: “For three sins of Gaza, even for four, I will not relent. Because she took captive whole communities and sold them to Edom,
Thus says the LORD: “For three transgressions of Gaza, and for four, I will not revoke the punishment, because they carried into exile a whole people to deliver them up to Edom.
The words of Amos, one of the shepherds of Tekoa—the vision he saw concerning Israel two years before the earthquake, when Uzziah was king of Judah and Jeroboam son of Jehoash was king of Israel.

The words of Amos, who was among the shepherds of Tekoa, which he saw concerning Israel in the days of Uzziah king of Judah and in the days of Jeroboam the son of Joash, king of Israel, two years before the earthquake.

thread.text: 'The LORD will roar from Zion' — identical language, suggesting a shared prophetic tradition of divine war imagery.
The reign of Jeroboam II and the prophecy of Jonah, placing Amos in the same historical moment.
Hazael's atrocities against Gilead, the specific crime condemned in the Damascus oracle.
Another oracle against the Philistine cities, including Gaza, pronouncing doom on the coastal powers.
The treaty between Solomon and Hiram of Tyre — the 'covenant of brotherhood' that Tyre violated.
The most extensive prophetic condemnation of Edom's fraternal treachery, sharing themes with the Amos oracle.

coaching.tip: Amos is a 'herdsman from Tekoa' — no guild prophet, no court chaplain. God takes a shepherd from Judah and sends him north to Israel. This outsider status matters: Amos can't be dismissed as self-interested. 'The LORD took me from following the flock' (7:15). The messenger's credentials are his lack of credentials.

The oracles against nations follow a pattern: Damascus, Gaza, Tyre, Edom, Ammon, Moab — Israel's neighbors judged for war crimes. The audience would have applauded. Then Judah (2:4), getting closer. Then Israel (2:6) — the trap springs. The prophet builds false confidence before turning the sword.

'For three transgressions... and for four' — this formula (x, x+1) is common in wisdom literature (Proverbs 30:15-31). Amos is saying: the cup of sin is full and overflowing. One more transgression beyond the full count. God has been patient; that patience has limits. The formula itself is a theological claim.
